title: Chapter 10 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of refs and DOM manipulation!

questions.question: What does the `useRef` hook return?
options: A function to set the ref
A mutable object with a `.current` property
A reference to the component instance
questions.explanation: `useRef` returns an object with a `.current` property that can hold any value.

questions.question: Changing a ref's `.current` value causes the component to re-render.
questions.explanation: No, ref updates are silent – they don't trigger re-renders.

questions.question: How do you attach a ref to a DOM element?
questions.explanation: Use the special `ref` attribute and pass the ref object.

questions.question: When is the `.current` property of a ref populated with a DOM node?
options: After the component mounts (usually in `useEffect` or after render)
questions.explanation: The DOM node is assigned after the component mounts, so you can access it in `useEffect` or event handlers.

questions.question: Refs can only be used with DOM elements, not with class components.
questions.explanation: Refs can also be used on class components to get the component instance, but with function components you need `forwardRef`.

questions.question: What is a common use case for storing a value in a ref?
options: Keeping a mutable value that shouldn't cause re-renders
questions.explanation: All are valid use cases for refs as mutable containers.

questions.question: To expose a DOM node from a child component to a parent, you need to use `React._____`.
questions.explanation: `React.forwardRef` lets you pass a ref through to a child.

options: Store the number 5 in the ref without re-rendering
questions.explanation: It updates the ref's current value, but no re-render occurs.

questions.question: You can use a ref to call methods like `.focus()` on an input.
questions.explanation: Yes, once you have the DOM node, you can call its imperative methods.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a good use case for refs?
options: Storing the current value of an input (controlled component)
Integrating with a third-party library that manipulates the DOM
questions.explanation: For controlled components, you should use state and `onChange`. Refs are for imperative actions, not for managing input value.

questions.question: In a function component, the `useRef` hook returns an object with a single property: `_____`.
questions.explanation: The object has a `.current` property.

questions.question: What is the difference between `useRef` and creating `{ current: initial }` manually?
options: `useRef` gives you the same object on every render, while a manually created object would be recreated
`useRef` returns a function, the other returns an object
questions.explanation: `useRef` returns the same object reference across renders, so it's stable.

questions.question: You can use a ref to store a previous value and compare it in an effect.
questions.explanation: Yes, that's a common pattern: store previous value in a ref and compare with current.

questions.question: What does `React.forwardRef` do?
options: It forwards props to a child component
It allows a component to accept a `ref` prop and pass it to a child DOM element
It forwards events to a parent
questions.explanation: `forwardRef` lets your component receive a ref and pass it along to a child.

questions.question: To focus an input after a button click, you can call `inputRef.current._____()`.
questions.explanation: The DOM method to focus is `.focus()`.

questions.question: If you need to expose more than just the DOM node (like custom imperative methods) from a component, you would use:
questions.explanation: `useImperativeHandle` customizes the instance value exposed to parent refs.

questions.question: A ref can be used to store a timer ID, and you can clear it in a cleanup function.
questions.explanation: Yes, storing the ID in a ref allows you to access it in cleanup.
The ref's current was updated to 2, so it logs 2.

questions.question: When would you NOT use a ref?
options: To read the latest value of a variable inside an effect without re-running the effect
To store a value that should persist across renders but not cause re-renders
To trigger a re-render when the value changes
To access a DOM node's dimensions
questions.explanation: If you need a re-render when the value changes, you should use state, not a ref.

questions.question: You can attach a ref to a function component only if it uses `React.forwardRef`.
questions.explanation: Yes, by default function components don't accept refs; you must wrap them in `forwardRef`.
